items.title: Exotoxin A
items.contentHtml:
Exotoxin A

  • Exotoxin A is an ADP-ribosyltransferase that inactivates elongation factor 2 (EF-2), inhibiting cellular protein synthesis, similar to the mechanism of diphtheria toxin.
  • It produces tissue necrosis and acute liver damage in disseminated infections.
  • Its toxA gene is regulated by the quorum sensing; expression increases under conditions of oxidative stress.
  • It is not neutralized by diphtheria antitoxin; treatment is specific antibiotic againstP. aeruginosa.
  • In animal models, anti-exotoxin A antibodies reduce mortality, but it is not used in routine clinical practice.

items.title: Intrinsic resistance to beta-lactams
items.contentHtml:
Intrinsic resistance to beta-lactams

P. aeruginosa presents intrinsic resistance to multiple beta-lactams due to the chromosomal production of inducible AmpC β-lactamase and the low permeability of its outer membrane (porin OprD).

  • AmpC hydrolyzes penicillins, 1st/2nd generation cephalosporins and cephamycins; does not affect ceftazidime, cefepime or carbapenems (except if derepression mutations are present).
  • Loss of OprD confers resistance to imipenem, but not to meropenem.
  • Frequent association with efflux pumps (MexAB-OprM) that increase resistance to β-lactams and others groups.

items.title: Biofilms in cystic fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Biofilm formation in cystic fibrosis

In cystic fibrosis, P. aeruginosa adopts a mucoid phenotype due to overexpression of algD (alginate production), forming biofilms that resist phagocytosis and antibiotics.

  • The mature biofilm presents water channels and oxygen gradients; the bacteria inside are in stationary phase and are tolerant to aminoglycosides and quinolones.
  • The quorum sensing (Las and Rhl systems) regulates the production of alginate and virulence factors such as pyocyanin.
  • Treatment: combination of inhaled tobramycin + aztreonam or oral ciprofloxacin; in severe exacerbations, ceftazidime + tobramycin IV.
  • New therapies: inhaled colistimethate and fosfomycin as antibiofilm.

items.foco: Most common cause of malignant otitis externa
items.title: Malignant external otitis
items.contentHtml:
Malignant otitis externa

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common cause of malignant otitis externa (MOE), a necrotizing infection of the external auditory canal that extends to the base of the skull, almost exclusive in elderly diabetics and immunocompromised patients.

  • Clinic: intense otalgia, purulent otorrhea, paralysis of the facial nerve (VII nerve) if there is osteomyelis of the temporal bone.
  • Diagnosis: CT or MRI shows erosion of the temporal bone; secretion culture confirmsP. aeruginosa.
  • Treatment: ceftazidime or cefepime IV 2 g every 8 hours for 6-8 weeks; Oral or IV ciprofloxacin is added if there is resistance or allergy.
  • Surgical debridement if there is a bone abscess or sequestration.

items.foco: Treatment of choice: ceftazidime or cefepime
items.title: Treatment of choice: ceftazidime or cefepime
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of choice: ceftazidime or cefepime

For severe infections due to P. aeruginosa sensitive, the treatment of choice is ceftazidime or cefepime, both 3rd/4th generation cephalosporins with antipseudomonal activity.

  • Ceftazidime: dose 2 g IV every 8h; does not cover gram-positive or anaerobic cocci.
  • Cefepime: 2 g IV every 8 hours; better coverage for Enterobacterales and gram-positive cocci (except MRSA).
  • In monotherapy, risk of development of resistance due to induction of AmpC (lower with cefepime).
  • Alternatives: piperacillin-tazobactam (4.5 g IV every 6h), meropenem (1 g IV every 8 hours) or imipenem (500 mg IV every 6 hours).

items.title: efflux pumps
items.contentHtml:
Resistance mechanism: efflux pumps

P. aeruginosa expresses efflux pumps of the RND (Resistance-Nodulation-Division) family that expel antibiotics, reducing their intracellular concentration.

  • The most relevant is MexAB-OprM, which confers resistance to β-lactams (except carbapenems), quinolones, tetracyclines and chloramphenicol.
  • MexXY-OprM: associated with resistance to aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin, amikacin) and macrolides.
  • MexCD-OprJ: overexpressed by mutations in nfxB; gives resistance to quinolones and 4th generation cephalosporins (cefepime).
  • Overexpression of efflux pumps is a mechanism of acquired resistance common in multidrug-resistant nosocomial strains.

items.title: Nosocomial infection in burn patients
items.contentHtml:
Nosocomial infection in burn patients

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the main cause of nosocomial infection in burn patients, colonizing the eschar and causing cellulitis, sepsis and necrotizing fasciitis.

  • Risk factors: loss of skin barrier, immunosuppression, use of catheters and ventilation mechanical.
  • Clinic: green exudate (pyocyanin), sweet odor, necrosis of the eschar and septicemia with ecthyma gangrenosum (violaceous skin lesions with necrotic center due to vascular invasion).
  • Empirical treatment: ceftazidime or cefepime + tobramycin or amikacin; in resistant strains, sodium colistimethate (colistin) IV.
  • Early surgical debridement and coverage with silver dressings (silver sulfadiazine).

items.title: Quorum sensing and pyocyanin
items.contentHtml:
Quorum sensing and pyocyanin production

The quorum sensing (QS) in P. aeruginosa is mediated by two main systems: LasI/LasR (3-oxo-C12-HSL autoinducer) and RhlI/RhlR (C4-HSL), which regulate the expression of virulence factors, including pyocyanin.

  • Pyocyanin: blue-green phenazine pigment, acts as a toxin that generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), damaging the respiratory epithelium and competing with the mitochondrial respiratory chain.
  • Pyocyanin inhibits ciliary function and phagocytosis, favoring chronicity in cystic fibrosis.
  • QS also regulates the formation of biofilms and the production of exotoxin A, proteases and alginate.
  • Anti-QS drugs under investigation (e.g. furanones, azithromycin in subinhibitory doses).

items.title: Sensitive to aminoglycosides and quinolones
items.contentHtml:
Sensitive to aminoglycosides and quinolones

P. aeruginosa is usually sensitive to aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin, amikacin) and quinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin), although acquired resistance is increasing.

  • Aminoglycosides: they bind to the 30S ribosomal subunit; resistance occurs due to modifying enzymes (AAC, APH, ANT) or MexXY efflux pumps.
  • Ciprofloxacin: dose 400 mg IV every 12 hours or 500-750 mg PO every 12 hours; resistance emerges due to mutations in DNA gyrase (gyrA) and topoisomerase IV (parC), and by overexpression of efflux pumps.
  • In uncomplicated urinary infections, ciprofloxacin is the first line; in systemic infections it is used in combination.
  • Do not use monotherapy with aminoglycoside for pneumonia due to low pulmonary penetration.

items.foco: Cause of pneumonia on mechanical ventilation
items.title: Pneumonia on mechanical ventilation
items.contentHtml:
Pneumonia on mechanical ventilation

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common cause of pneumonia on mechanical ventilation among non-fermenting gram-negative bacilli, especially in ICU and prolonged stays.

  • Risk factors: previous use of antibiotics, COPD, immunosuppression, re-intubation.
  • Diagnosis: quantitative lavage culture bronchoalveolar (BAL) >10⁴ CFU/mL or protected brush >10³ CFU/mL.
  • Empirical treatment: combination of two antipseudomonal agents: ceftazidime or cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam + tobramycin or ciprofloxacin; in allergies, aztreonam + amikacin.
  • Duration: 7-14 days; de-escalate according to antibiogram.

items.title: Positive oxidase test
items.contentHtml:
Positive oxidase test (gram-negative bacillus)

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a strict aerobic, non-lactose-fermenting gram-negative bacillus that presents a positive oxidase test, key for its presumptive identification in the microbiology laboratory.

  • Oxidase detects the presence of cytochrome c oxidase; P. aeruginosa is oxidase-positive, differentiating it from enterobacteria (oxidase-negative).
  • Other oxidase-positive gram-negative bacilli: Burkholderia cepacia, Stenotrophomonas maltophilia (the latter is oxidase-weak or negative).
  • Colonies: grape odor (aminoacetophenone), hemolysis on blood agar, green pigment (pyocyanin) or blue-green (pyocyanin + pyoverdine).
  • Does not ferment lactose on MacConkey agar; produces colorless colonies.

items.title: Combination treatment for serious infections
items.contentHtml:
Combined treatment for severe infections

In severe infections due to P. aeruginosa (sepsis, pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation, bacteremia in neutropenics), combined treatment with two active drugs for synergy and prevention of resistance is recommended.

  • Standard regimen: antipseudomonal β-lactam (ceftazidime, cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem) + aminoglycoside (tobramycin 5-7 mg/kg/day or amikacin 15-20 mg/kg/day).
  • Alternative: β-lactam + ciprofloxacin (400 mg IV every 12 hours).
  • In multidrug-resistant strains (MDR/XDR): colistin (colistimethate sodium 9 million IU load, then 4.5 million IU every 12h) + β-lactam or fosfomycin.
  • Monotherapy only in mild or uncomplicated urinary infections.
